Details: Comparison of two test case generation outputs for BigQuery customer purchase analysis system. Agent_1_Output provides 10 test cases with basic pytest implementation, while Agent_2_Output delivers 15 comprehensive test cases with advanced pytest fixtures and helper functions. Agent_2_Output demonstrates superior coverage of edge cases, error handling, and production-ready test infrastructure.

Both outputs target the same core objective of testing customer purchase analysis functionality. Agent_1_Output covers fundamental scenarios (TC001-TC010) including basic calculations, tier assignments, and data validation. Agent_2_Output extends this with additional edge cases (TC01-TC15) covering NULL handling, boundary conditions, and error scenarios. The semantic alignment is strong but Agent_2_Output provides more comprehensive coverage of business logic edge cases.

Both follow pytest structure but with significant architectural differences. Agent_1_Output uses simple test functions with embedded SQL comments (lines 25-85). Agent_2_Output implements sophisticated test infrastructure with fixtures (lines 20-35), helper functions for table creation/deletion (lines 5-18), and parameterized setup. Agent_1_Output has flat structure while Agent_2_Output uses layered architecture with setup/teardown patterns.

Valid pytest syntax throughout. Test functions properly structured with assert statements. However, SQL query placeholders are incomplete (comment-only, lines 25-85). BigQuery client initialization is correct (line 5). Missing actual query implementation reduces practical usability.

Excellent pytest implementation with proper fixture usage, schema definitions, and comprehensive error handling. Complete table creation/deletion logic (lines 5-18). Proper BigQuery client integration with dataset management. Minor issue: file path reference 'customer_purchase_query.txt' (line 45) assumes external file existence without validation.

Average correctness score reflecting Agent_2_Output's superior implementation quality offset by Agent_1_Output's incomplete query integration. Both demonstrate solid pytest fundamentals with Agent_2_Output showing production-ready practices.

Complete the SQL query implementations in test functions. Add fixture-based setup for consistent test data management. Implement table creation/deletion helpers for test isolation. Consider parameterized tests for tier boundary testing.

Add validation for external SQL file existence before reading. Consider embedding critical queries directly in tests for better portability. Add more detailed assertion messages for debugging failed tests. Consider adding performance benchmarks for large dataset scenarios.
